content.text: Why GenAI On-Call Differs from Standard Software On-Call

On-call rotation design for GenAI services has a structural difference from traditional software services: the failure modes split along two distinct skill boundaries. Infrastructure failures — provider outages, latency spikes, availability drops — are the territory of an SRE engineer who understands distributed systems, circuit breakers, and rollback procedures. Model quality failures — eval pass rate regression, safety flag rate spikes, prompt behavior drift — require an ML engineer who can read the eval dashboard, compare model versions, and diagnose whether a degradation is in the model, the prompt, or the evaluation harness itself.

A single-tier on-call rotation forces engineers to be competent in both domains. In practice, SRE engineers get paged for eval quality regressions they cannot diagnose, and ML engineers get paged for infrastructure outages they cannot fix. The standard solution is a two-tier rotation: SRE primary handles all first-response and infrastructure triage, while ML secondary is escalated to specifically for quality, safety, and model-layer issues.

attrs.alt: Two-tier on-call rotation structure with SRE primary and ML secondary, weekly handoff checklist, and escalation flow

content.text: Business hours primary; async backup for off-hours safety incidents
ML engineers and senior prompt engineers (minimum 3)
Availability, latency, budget burn, infra alerts
Eval quality regression, safety flag spikes, model version issues
Engineering Manager for P1 prolonged incidents
SRE primary if the issue is infra-layer, not model-layer

Minimum rotation sizes: below 4 engineers in the SRE rotation, each engineer is on-call every 4 weeks — which is considered the maximum sustainable frequency by Google's SRE book. With 3 engineers, on-call every 3 weeks produces burnout within months. If the team is below minimum, either hire before launching the on-call program or accept reduced 24/7 coverage with documented risk.
Manage rotation schedules programmatically so that changes are version-controlled and the current on-call is always queryable by automated systems (deploy gates, Slack bots, budget alert routing).

# rotation_config.py — PagerDuty rotation management via API
import os
import requests
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone

PD_API_KEY = os.environ['PAGERDUTY_API_KEY']
HEADERS = {
    'Authorization': f'Token token={PD_API_KEY}',
    'Content-Type': 'application/json',
    'Accept': 'application/vnd.pagerduty+json;version=2',
}

def get_current_oncall(schedule_id: str) -> dict:
    """Returns the current on-call user for a schedule."""
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
    resp = requests.get(
        f'https://api.pagerduty.com/schedules/{schedule_id}/users',
        headers=HEADERS,
        params={
            'since': now.isoformat(),
            'until': (now + timedelta(minutes=1)).isoformat(),
        },
        timeout=10,
    )
    users = resp.json().get('users', [])
    return users[0] if users else {}

def get_oncall_schedule_week(schedule_id: str) -> list[dict]:
    """Returns the full on-call schedule for the next 7 days."""
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
    resp = requests.get(
        f'https://api.pagerduty.com/schedules/{schedule_id}',
        headers=HEADERS,
        params={
            'since': now.isoformat(),
            'until': (now + timedelta(days=7)).isoformat(),
        },
        timeout=10,
    )
    schedule = resp.json().get('schedule', {})
    final_schedule = schedule.get('final_schedule', {})
    return final_schedule.get('rendered_schedule_entries', [])

def post_weekly_oncall_to_slack(
    sre_schedule_id: str,
    ml_schedule_id: str,
    slack_webhook: str,
) -> None:
    sre = get_current_oncall(sre_schedule_id)
    ml  = get_current_oncall(ml_schedule_id)
    message = (
        '*GenAI On-Call This Week*\n'
        f'  :pager: SRE Primary: {sre.get("name", "unknown")}\n'
        f'  :brain: ML Secondary: {ml.get("name", "unknown")}\n'
        '\nFor P1/P2 incidents, page SRE primary first.'
    )
    requests.post(slack_webhook, json={'text': message}, timeout=5)

The handoff is the moment of highest risk in any rotation. An incoming on-call engineer who does not know that budget is at 18% remaining, or that a P2 incident is still under investigation, is set up to fail in their first hour. The handoff must be synchronous for active incidents and must include a structured checklist that is verified, not just shared.
Run the handoff check script before the Thursday handoff meeting. It blocks rotation completion if open incidents or low budget require explicit acknowledgment.
Budget status: outgoing on-call reads current remaining for all SLOs and freeze status (5 min)
Open incidents: each P1/P2 ticket reviewed — status, who owns it, expected resolution (10 min)
Scheduled deploys this week: any high-risk releases with the deploy type and contact (5 min)
Known fragility: any subsystem in degraded state with workarounds and runbook links (5 min)
Async summary posted to #oncall-handoff Slack channel within 30 minutes of meeting end (5 min)

Every alert type must have a defined escalation path before the on-call program launches. Ambiguity about who to page for an eval quality regression at 2:00 AM is resolved in advance by the escalation matrix, not in the moment by the on-call engineer under pressure.

# escalation_matrix.py — returns the right escalation contact for a given alert
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class AlertCategory(Enum):
    AVAILABILITY   = 'availability'
    LATENCY        = 'latency'
    EVAL_QUALITY   = 'eval_quality'
    SAFETY         = 'safety'
    BUDGET_BURN    = 'budget_burn'
    INFRA          = 'infra'

@dataclass
class EscalationPath:
    tier1: str      # PagerDuty schedule ID for SRE
    tier2: str      # PagerDuty schedule ID for ML secondary (or None)
    sla_ack_minutes: int
    notes: str

ESCALATION_MATRIX: dict[AlertCategory, EscalationPath] = {
    AlertCategory.AVAILABILITY: EscalationPath(
        tier1='sre-primary',
        tier2=None,
        sla_ack_minutes=15,
        notes='Check provider status first; availability is usually upstream',
    ),
    AlertCategory.LATENCY: EscalationPath(
        tier1='sre-primary',
        tier2=None,
        sla_ack_minutes=15,
        notes='Check model version and request concurrency before escalating',
    ),
    AlertCategory.EVAL_QUALITY: EscalationPath(
        tier1='sre-primary',
        tier2='ml-secondary',
        sla_ack_minutes=30,
        notes='SRE acknowledges; ML secondary investigates root cause in eval dashboard',
    ),
    AlertCategory.SAFETY: EscalationPath(
        tier1='sre-primary',
        tier2='ml-secondary',
        sla_ack_minutes=15,
        notes='Disable feature flag immediately; page ML secondary regardless of hour',
    ),
    AlertCategory.BUDGET_BURN: EscalationPath(
        tier1='sre-primary',
        tier2=None,
        sla_ack_minutes=30,
        notes='SRE gates deploys; PM notified async for burn at 6x+',
    ),
    AlertCategory.INFRA: EscalationPath(
        tier1='sre-primary',
        tier2=None,
        sla_ack_minutes=15,
        notes='Platform team secondary if infra is outside SRE scope',
    ),
}

def get_escalation(category: AlertCategory) -> EscalationPath:
    return ESCALATION_MATRIX[category]
Yes — page ML regardless of hour

Safety alerts are the exception to the 'Tier 1 first, Tier 2 only on escalation' rule. A safety flag rate spike is paged to both tiers simultaneously because the first mitigation (disable feature flag) is SRE-executable, but the root cause diagnosis (which prompt pattern is triggering flags) requires ML context immediately.

An on-call program that is not measured will degrade. Engineers will underreport toil, runbook gaps will accumulate, and the rotation will become a source of burnout rather than a reliability mechanism. Measure on-call health weekly and review the metrics in each monthly SRE retrospective.
Audit alert thresholds; suppress noisy alerts until fixed
Block new alert rule deployments until runbook exists for each
Reliability sprint: reliability work takes priority over features
Outgoing on-call retains pager until incident is resolved or handed synchronously
Check PD escalation policy; secondary may fire too late

Eval quality alerts are often slow to fire because the eval pass rate uses a 7-day rolling window. By the time an alert fires, the degradation may be 3–4 days old. Train ML secondary on-call to interpret the trend direction, not just the current value.

Provider incidents are not fixable by the on-call engineer. The runbook action for an LLM provider outage is: activate fallback model or queue, update status page, monitor for recovery. Do not page ML secondary for pure provider outages — they cannot help.

Prompt changes pushed via config management bypass standard deploy monitoring. Add a Slack notification to your config management pipeline that posts prompt changes to #oncall-handoff so the current on-call knows to watch for eval quality shifts.

Model version updates from the provider (when using non-pinned API versions) can change latency and quality distributions silently. The on-call runbook must include how to pin a specific model version and when to do so.
Single-tier rotation with all engineers covering all alert types: ML engineers burn out on infra pages; SRE engineers burn out on model quality issues they cannot diagnose
On-call rotation launched without runbooks for every alert type: the first on-call shift becomes a runbook-writing exercise during an incident
Handoff done async-only via Slack message: incoming on-call does not read the message before their first page; synchronous handoff for any open incident is mandatory
No measurement of off-hours interruptions: toil accumulates invisibly until engineers resign or quietly stop acknowledging pages promptly

ML secondary on 24/7 pager for eval quality: eval quality degradations are almost never true emergencies that require a 3 AM response; business-hours coverage with async Slack escalation for off-hours is appropriate

GenAI services require a two-tier on-call model because their failure modes split across infrastructure (SRE domain) and model quality/safety (ML engineer domain). The SRE primary rotation runs weekly on a 24/7 pager with a minimum of four engineers. The ML secondary rotation runs bi-weekly with business-hours primary coverage, paged only for eval quality, safety, and model-layer escalations. Structured weekly handoffs with a validated checklist prevent knowledge gaps at rotation boundaries. An escalation matrix defined before launch removes ambiguity about who to page for each alert type. Measure on-call health weekly — off-hours interruption rate, runbook gap rate, and time to acknowledge — and treat the on-call program itself as a system that requires reliability investment.
